title: Amy Piazza -Panel Interview | Events Marketing Manager
meeting_title: Amy Piazza -Panel Interview | Events Marketing Manager

transcript.text: Doing well.
How are you?
Pretty good.
Back-to-back today, but those are usually my Wednesdays.
My Wednesdays are usually the back-to-back day.
The Wednesdays are crazy.
Yeah, well, appreciate you guys making the time, fitting me in here.
Yeah, likewise.
I'm excited to connect.
Yeah, definitely.
So we have, I think, the stuff you sent over.
Maybe we could just do quick intros.
It's the first time we're all meeting.

And then, yeah, for the, like, the kind of assignment, you know, presentation part itself, like, we keep it really casual, you know, but obviously, if you can walk through it and then give us a chance to ask questions, we can just take it from there.
Yeah, but intros-wise, I mean, my name's Jason.
I lead growth here at GrowthX.
I gave you a little context for what the marketing side means, you know, Marcel, I basically told her it's just what you do organically, but scaled up, essentially.
Nice dinners, you know, being an incredible human, basically.
And then the other half I spend with kind of some of our bigger customers, like Webflow, AI company called Surge, which you might have heard of, and Augment Code, and a bunch of other ones.
And my background's basically doing growth for a bunch of different startups.
I had my own founder of my own DevTools company out of YC, and I met Marcel actually at an event almost a year ago, or sorry, not almost a year ago.
Actually, yeah, no, almost a year ago, last September, last October.
And when I learned what he was working on, I was like, this is a no-brainer, and I joined him a few months after.
Amazing.
Thank you for sharing.
Yeah, I know.
I can go super quickly, but I'm a CEO here, co-founder, and been at different companies, you know, my trajectory, like from IBM, HP.
And eventually like different startups, like HashiCorp, Service Titan, Scale.
And I think it was a lot of my time at Scale, I got into doing a lot of events.
did three virtual conferences in 18 months.
First one was like 10,000 people, second was 23, and then the third one was 35,000 people virtually.
So I like, both the whole team did it in a very scrappy way, but it was like really cool.
And then we were running a lot of these super high-end events as well.
We had like CTO of Meta, know, like Eric Schmidt, like these types of people like coming to a lot of these events, you know, super high-end.
And so I just paid a lot of dividends and also doing a lot of community events.
And so I think it was the first time where I had to run events as part of what I did.

And so not only gain a lot of appreciation for everything, the A to Z, but also how to do it in a different way versus like the standard playbook I had seen in other companies that I was never like a huge fan.
Fan of.
And so I just kind of like carry that with me, completely reinvented the events program at DeepGram as well.
And that became like, I think one of the highest ROI things we were doing.
And then here, pretty much from day one doing that, you know, the company started with virtual workshops that then people paid 500 bucks to come to for a half day.
And then that's how we got our first two customers, then another workshop, got another six and then eight and then 12.
And then, you know, so we did after about 170 people paid to come to these workshops, the company was like, multi-million dollar in revenue or like, okay, this works.
And then started doing breakfast for CMOs and then hosting dinners.
And, you know, at first I got VCs to pay for the dinners because we didn't have a lot of VC money.
And then we raised our A and started doing it for real.
And then I think now we're at a point where it's like, we really need to mature everything we're doing, you know, and the company's doing really well.
And that's exciting to kind of hear a bit more on your thoughts on it too.
Yeah, absolutely.
Well, thank you guys so much for all that context.
So helpful and amazing to hear from you guys.
I do have a slide in my deck where I'll dive deeper into my background and what I've been doing.
Essentially, Amy Robertson Piazza, I've been working in the events industry for about five years now and really excited.
I was very excited when I received the prompt to get working on this.
So I'm excited to share.
Awesome.
Let's do it.
Cool.
Okay.
Let me share my screen.
Can you guys see it?
Yep.
Yeah, yeah, absolutely.
And feel free to go in like the, you know, as strong pace as you want to.
I, as Jason knows, I talk super fast.
So it's like, there's no such thing as too fast, I guess is what I'm saying.
That really doesn't talk to me, not for me.
It's like slow down.
So good to know.
Yeah.
Okay.
GrowthX events.
Like I said, thank you guys for your time.
Was really excited when I received the prompt.
For this presentation, I'm happy to be walking you guys through kind of my visions, my execution process, and just plans in the context of your current event strategy.
Feel free to stop me, ask questions, or we can save that for the end, whatever works best for you guys.
Cool.
Okay, so just so that we all have context and know what to expect, I'm going to go ahead and start with my background and move into your guys' current event strategy.
Then kind of walk through my execution process that I typically follow.
I'm going to break down some ideas by event, look at how we're going to evaluate success, and then go through how I see my first 30 days looking like in the role.
Okay, so who is Amy Robertson Piazza?
Like I said, I've been working in the events industry for about five years now.
Most recently at Anthropic on the internal events team.
So working on all employees.

We engagement initiatives, everything from small-scale team off-site, social hours, internal hackathons, all the way up to the large-scale global initiatives like our company holiday program, our company-wide off-site, and then supporting any external events happening within the office space from the logistics side.
Prior to this, I was working at Andreessen Horowitz for about three years, working on events for the Growth Fund for the two founders, Mark and Ben, and also one of the LP Summits.
So that included a lot of C-level executive dinners, executive retreats, some webinar series sprinkled in there as well.
At the beginning of my career, I started off in production, first at George P.
Johnson, on the speaker management side of those large-scale technology conferences, and then later at Creative Edge in New York, working on more social programs.
So, graduated Auburn in 2018, big SEC football.
Fan, basketball more often these days or having better luck.
So yeah, that's a little bit about me.
Okay, so from what I understand based on the prompt and previous interviews that I've done, I understand that your strategy really consists of four different event types right now.
So CMO breakfast every one to two months, an executive dinner every couple of months, joint happy hours as the industry allows, and then a goal of a user conference.
In San Francisco.
So these are great opportunities, a clear roadmap to, I'm excited to work on and build upon and hit the ground running on.
Okay, I wanted to provide a visual to just show you guys what that six month event calendar could look like based on the event type and frequency that you're hoping to accomplish.
I think it'd be good to start with a CMO breakfast, low lift, really get me ramped up, understand the audience.
You know.
Yeah.
Understand the event culture at GrowthX and go ahead and get started in that executive dinner in the second month.
Sprinkle in those happy hours where it's fit.
And then this also gives us great lead time to produce a really special exceptional experience for that user conference at the end of the term.
When it comes to my execution process, I mean, it really, the baseline remains the same.
It just scales up depending on the event.

So pre-event, stakeholder, speaker, host, management, and support, meeting with you guys, aligning on preparation materials, goals, everything for the event, those comprehensive resource builds, resource documents, registration, guest communication builds.
Of course, securing any venues and vendors and working with them to ensure expectations are set and met.
And then, bye.
Budget tracking and when-wanted gifting curation.
Day of, I manage all aspects of the event on the ground, you know, ensuring a smooth and great experience for the guests, starting at that first contact at registration and welcoming them.
I act as the point person for all of the vendors and support staff and direct the flow, execution and timeline of the event, and troubleshoot in real time as necessary.
I'll support the host and any stakeholders with direction on-site, and then capturing content, whether for sharing or just for documentation and reflection in the future.
Post event is all about follow-ups and future improvements.
So the day after, starting with those thank you messages, as you mentioned doing, Jason.
Sending our thank yous, collecting feedback through the surveys, distributing any content that we see fit.
Analyzing performance.
performance.
Which I will cover more on in a later slide.
Let's have a stakeholder debrief meeting to go over, you know, what went well from their point of view.
And from there, we document all of this and make improvements for the next event.
I wanted to share.
On maybe the pre-event as well, especially like given the, I love your experience as well.
Like, how do you think about the inviting, like more executives?
What are the things you saw like work?
Yeah.
Like a decent network, but also we're constantly trying to expand that network.
And so, you know, curious on what has worked and how you get those, you know, invites.
And then also on the other end of that is like, you know, in smaller, more intimate events, how do you make sure people show up when you're paying a thousand dollars a seat?
You know, if someone doesn't show up, you know.

Yeah, I mean, from what I've seen in previous events, you know, I think having like a personal email to these executives from someone that they recognize rather than one of those like pre-built emails is definitely something that's worked a lot better versus, you know, just sending them a mass email.
So maybe it's you, Marcel, or you, Jason, would send the invitation email and CC me so that I can like support anything moving forward.
So that's something that we've done in the past that definitely created like a more intimate experience and made them feel more connected to the hosts and the stakeholders.
Okay, I wanted to show some examples of those resources that I talked about in the last side, specifically that workbook that I'm showing on the left side of the page.
So this is a really comprehensive document that I built out for all of my events and it'll house everything from the workbook schedule.
So attendee lists, agendas, seating charts, budget tracking, all the things.
And this is really helpful in alignment across the team because you guys can easily find any information that you're looking for.
And it also holds me accountable and keeps me organized.
I saw that you guys are using Luma typically for your registration site, which I know is really popular.
So I'm happy to work out of that for the registration builds, or I've previously also worked in SWUGO and Splash that.
And then, of course, those guest communications and post-event surveys that I spoke about.
I also wanted to share what a detailed workback calendar looks like from my point of view, specifically for an executive dinner.
So I put in an eight-week workback calendar.
I do these for all of my events.
It really helps me stay organized when juggling multiple events and multiple priorities.发ib're current activities.
Right?
Dr.
Make sure nothing slips through the cracks and provides broader visibility about what has and has not been accomplished.
I don't want to spend too much time going through the weeds of this with you guys, but as you can see, it has all of the little details down to place card setting.
So just to make sure that I'm accountable and everything is accounted for for the event.
Okay, I wanted to dive in starting with the executive dinners on just my vision and some ideas I have for each of your event types.
So for the executive dinners, I really see these as an intimate roundtable over an amazing meal, really offering deepening relationships with the people that we already have a connection with.
So sharing insights in a like-minded environment, elevating the brand, and establishing that thought leadership influence.
So these type of events typically span three hours with a welcome reception, seated.
And then I prefer a standing dessert salon to encourage further conversation and networking.
And what I see as making these valuable to people, it will be the facilitated discussion.
So we'll leverage your guys' network and our leadership team to really create a program with some learning and connection opportunities there.

You know, in my experience, I think having a special person that people would like to learn from or meet has been a topic that's interesting and relevant is really what sets these type of events apart in a guest's mind from just another like nice dinner at a nice restaurant, which is happening every day.
So, but that being said, of course, the baseline is an amazing venue and amazing meal.
So I've included some venue ideas of places that I've either worked before or that I'm dying to work at.
So like Oficina, Birdsong, Marianne, Speakeasy, and I've even done some really elevated and lovely.
Dinner series in an office space, working with Foxtel and Kampanair before that I think that we could even do at 95 Federal.
Cool.
I mean, just on the like facilitated round two part, could you go a little bit more?
Yeah, this was something that we did.
Yeah.
So at A16Z, this was something where essentially we were doing CXO dinner series.
So the COO of the growth fund would coordinate these dinners with CMOs, CEOs, CFOs, et cetera.

And they would essentially bring in an internal expert on those items and they would have a facilitated discussion with the group about like different topics that were relevant to them right now, how you could offer support to each other and create connections and chat groups within those places so that the conversation could continue on afterwards.
Does that make sense?
Yep.
That sounds good.
I mean, I think that was just fresh in my mind because we...
On Monday, we kind of did this like a little ad hoc and one thing in my head was like, yeah, it would have been good to like have a more deliberate itinerary.
I definitely think so because like you said, like how do you get the people there who have so many invitations?
I think it's having that relevant topic and having a discussion that's relevant to them and having those people there to facilitate that that could benefit them.
Okay, moving on to CMO breakfast, where the executive dinner was all about elevation and intimacy.
I really see these differently.
So I see these as a easily templatable and scalable opportunity to expand our community, create new connections, build on our credibility and just capture some fun content.
So I see this as like a welcome to our home type experience, a casual 90 minute networking connection opportunity over a cup of coffee or juice or a breakfast.

So I love the idea of having a recurring and evolving guest list where we invite our guests and maybe offer them a bring a friend opportunity to broaden our network and expand reach and get those new contacts.

So like I said, I think this is easily scalable and repeatable, and we could easily turn this into like a once a month breakfast club type of situation based off of, of course, your interest in hosting those.
Um, we'll bring catering into the office, creating like a cafe environment and support our local community vendors through activations and specialty moments.
Maybe like you can see here, a co-branded juice bar or some fun swag to show off that you have been to the office.
I mean, we, we did one, I can't remember how long ago now, like three weeks ago.
It was like the first one in our new office, which houses up to like 50, I think it's like 50 or 60.
Um, and it was really good.
But I think like for our events, it always feels like we do them two, three weeks, last minute, you know, grab everybody to come to the event.
So I guess are you suggesting like, I don't know, to almost like be like, hey, first Tuesday of every month, like we're doing this and almost to make it like feel a little bit more.
If feels feasible to you guys, I think it would be a great idea because then it's something that the audience and network can like plan for each month and expect for.
So yeah, maybe what is the first or second Tuesday of each month, you know, that these breakfasts are happening.
And like, if you're in town, you plan on coming and seeing everyone.
That's how I see it, at least.
Got it.
And then I know Marcel asked already, but like for this one, you know, you can fit quite a few more people.
Just like tactically, any ways to like expand past our initial audience, like to get, let's say like every event we wanted, at least, you know, like 10 to 15 new.
You know, that kind of fit our ICP.
Like, what would you try to do to make that happen?
Yeah.

Well, like I said, I think it's a good idea here to offer a bring a friend or bring a colleague option where people can bring people from either within their team or someone within the industry that is relevant to this.
And that would be a way that we could bring more people into our office, make connections with them and receive their information for future.
Got it.
And do you have any experience to kind of just like using more like demand gen type tools?

Like, let's say, you know, like we have a decent brand now and we just want every CMO that kind of is in the Bay Area to kind of know about us, you know, like if we want to reach those people, do you kind of have any thoughts for how we could try to do that?
To be honest, most of my events, working at Anthropic, it's an internal audience.
So we obviously had all of theirs.
Contact information.
in A16Z, it was an exclusive audience that was already within our portfolio for the most part.
So I don't have experience in that, but I'm happy to learn as I go.
Cool.
Anything else?
No, please continue.
Great.
Okay.

For these joint happy hours, I think this is a really great way to, build GrowthX's reputation, collaborate with clients, build off of pre-existing industry opportunity, and just become a fixture in the broader culture.
So I see this as a two-hour post-conference event that just really gives people the opportunity to relax and enjoy themselves after a long day in a probably stuffy conference center.
So I see this is a great opportunity to leverage our client or partners network for the invitation list to increase visibility.
and expand our reach again.
Thanks to San Francisco's great weather, I think an outdoor venue would be great for this, maybe Rooftop 25 or Dirty Habit, or again, we can leverage our office space.
So I see this is a fun social event that brings people together in a low commitment way to just further establish GrowthX as a player and increase industry engagement and visibility.
Got it.
So for, I guess on this, just to make it more real, we have one next week at 25 Lusk.
Oh, yeah, Rooftop 25, yeah.
Rooftop, yeah.
We have, I don't maybe like 120-ish registrants.
We're trying to get a lot higher because it's inbound conference, so no idea how many will actually show up.
I guess we're trying to think through just like what would make that experience a little bit more like memorable.
Because for the most part, I mean, we'll...
We'll try to have as many growthx people there as we can, but presumably we can't talk to every one of them, right?
But we want them to leave with like some impression of us, like any thoughts on how to do that?
Well, of course, I need to dive in deeper to like really understand the company and the goals there.
But I think that having like some sort of entertainment or some sort of like interesting food or beverage installation would be really interesting.
Do you guys have any specialty moments like that?
I think we're looking into a photo booth.
I think we're getting a wheel of some sort for like special swag.
Nice.
I that's it for now.
People do love swag.
I've noticed that that is a really, people are really excited about swag right now.
So that's a great idea.
And the photo booth is a good idea too, because then it offers like a shareable moment to increase engagement on social media.
So I think that that's great.
I think having some sort of.
Maybe a DJ or something would make it more of a fun environment and offer the people that you're not speaking to something to focus on outside of that conversation.
Cool.
I mean, just on Swag, we were previously working with this company called Canary.
And I mean, their stuff's decent, but it's like a little generic, I guess.
know, like how many hats can somebody own before they like just toss it?
You know, like any kind of things you've done with Swag before where it's like a little bit more memorable or like.
The most memorable thing I've done with Swag is not very scalable.
I'll be honest.
We got some custom Ralph Lauren puffers with like the logo embroidered and people were obsessed with those, obviously.
But I just also noticed like a little bit more muted and toned down on the branding is something people are liking.
Like in Anthropic, we did these really high quality T-shirts, which everyone does a T

But rather than some big logo across it, we did like a tonal embroidery in a small corner on the left side of the shoulder so that like it was kind of like you don't see it unless you're looking for it.
And people really loved that.
I think it's providing something that people actually like to see and wear and like have on them versus being like a walking billboard.
You get what I'm saying?
Yeah, mean, like just on that, like, let's say, I guess sometimes I don't really know how to think about swag.
Like we could spend 20 bucks on something small or like $200 on something big.
But like, how do you think about the goal of swag, you know, for an audience like ours?
It's like kind of marketing leaders, you know?
Yeah, I mean, marketing leaders don't need any swag.
So let's start there.
A lot of them don't even take it, I think, whenever they come to events.
But I have noticed like something that's usable, for example, at the executive dinners, we could do like.
A branded lovely notebook and like a branded really nice pen so that through the discussion they're taking notes and they can take that with them as they go.
So I think that thinking about the event and something that might be usable at the event is a great way to think about it.
Or maybe for the breakfast, it's a branded Tumblr that they can use to take their coffee to go as they leave the event.
I think having something that's actually useful while there and as they go is really important.
Okay, last but not least, the user conference.
Okay, this is really exciting to me.
I think it's a great opportunity to engage with and bring value to the partners, clients, as well as any prospects if we're including that.
I see this as like a six-hour format.
Start off with breakfast, do some morning sessions, lunch, afternoon sessions.
And...
And...
And then conclude the event with a really fun networking reception celebration.
So the focus here would definitely be on education and innovation.
We could leverage our internal and external speakers to have panelists to produce some really value-add content for the audience.
So this would take place at a great venue in San Francisco, preferably one with indoor and outdoor space.
I've worked at the Pearl before for something similar to this, and that was really great.
And I'm dying to work at the Conservatory at One Sansum because it's just such a gorgeous venue.
So this is definitely the largest scale event of the group with the most production.

So I think enlisting an agency partner here would be really beneficial to the execution and just ensuring we cover the AV, rentals, catering, on-site management, and, I mean, everything, the list goes on.
I have previously worked with Glow.
Grow and MSI agencies, who I think would be great options, who could really help hit this out of the park.
And see, this is creating a value-add program that also has some fun, surprise, and delight elements to really create an enjoyable experience for our partners.

I think about, like, budgeting, I think one of the things I kind of struggled with when I was on scale, too, is, like, did SFJS, and it was just, like, so hard to justify the ROI of, like, spending a million dollars on that for 500 people.
And it just balloons so quickly, and that's not even with, like, a networking event on top of that.
We ended up dropping a networking event at the end, and then it just felt like everybody was, like, craving to talk to other people.

And then, like, we missed out on it, you know, but mostly because, like, production and working with an agency ate up all the costs, pretty much, you know, so it's, like, the agency alone, and after we negotiated and everything.
It was essentially half a million dollars for a single day of that, you know.
Yeah, it definitely gets pricey.
I mean, we definitely can try to cut the agency, I will say, with like a very small team.
It helps a lot because we will need that on-site staff to at least be supporting with registration.
Like I said, all the AV, any security needs, all of that type of stuff.
So I typically do for the large, I've always worked on teams of two.
So I'm used to working on a small team.
But typically, we would enlist an agency for something with like this scale of production.
But dependent upon what you guys are really wanting to accomplish out of it and where the priorities are, then we can look at that.
Yeah, it's mostly like, here's kind of how we should think about it.
Like if you, like if we want to do an event for 150 people and it's 10 times the worth of hosting something for 50.
And it's 10 times the cost, it becomes really hard to do.
We want to do it.
think that's the thing we struggle with and why we kind of like pushed out.
We're going to do something in October.

And one, because we didn't have the team in place yet, but also like, but then like when we look back, although it was like this awesome, meaningful moment, the scale one that I did, it's like relative to doing, you know, 15 executive dinners.
It was really, you know, like, and, you know, especially at a, you know, scale where we're at.
And so, but then I've also been to events that were more like 100, 150 people.
And like, there's one called PLGTM at Gallery 308.
It didn't feel like crazy polished, you know, but it was like super high value.
It was great networking.
And, you know, and then when I asked the organizers, like, yeah, it costs us about 120 K to do the whole thing for 100 people.
And they found ways to like, be more thoughtful about the cost, you know.
Like just to that start somewhere versus like starting with like a million dollar event and eating up the entire event's budget for the year kind of thing, you know?
Yeah, definitely.
mean, like I said, this is just my idea based off I hear conference and I think like screens everywhere and major production.
That's definitely optional.
You know, it's like this is your company.
This is the scale that you want it to be.
And we we can accomplish what the goals are at whatever scale.
So I see it that way.
That makes sense.
Are there are there like areas of how we should think about it as far as like things to do an experience that's still like really feels great?
But or one another way to think about it is like, how do we keep the cost per attendee to not balloon?
You know, crazy, you know, is there like a sweet spot somewhere where it's just like if we do this or do that, like we can achieve like the best.
best value for for the experience.
Instead of the impact, you know, like where do you think is that right spot, sweet spot for a company?
Yeah, I mean, there's a lot of different aspects we could look at for that.
For example, like picking a venue that has a lot of already existing internal infrastructure.
It may not be the most amazing like AV production in the world.
It may not be the most gorgeous furniture, but like those type of things we could use in-house.
We could downgrade the, you know, like breakfast could be coffee instead.
Starting a little bit later in the day so that it's not a full breakfast production.
There are like tons of details that you can look into there that can really like scale it down by cost.
Like you said, it may not be an insane production, but it can still provide the option to share the content that you want to get out there and create a fun experience for people to connect on.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
So, yeah, like I think that the main thing is like even with small teens, what I found, like when you're doing.

Doing a massive production, recording a bunch of things, it definitely makes sense to use agencies, but then there's, like, a few agencies that are truly just doing a pass-through to, like, five other agencies, and then it's just, like, and then they have this massive staff, and it's just, like, feels like, okay, you're just being, playing telephone with us, like, you know.

Yeah, I mean, I've definitely had that experience before, where it's, like, okay, we work with the agency, and there's, like, a million other, like, people involved as well, so I understand what you're Yeah, it just feels like you're, it's very different than, like, someone that's, like, truly trying to shape the experience, and do this, and do that, you know, like, so, so it's mostly, mostly that, but, but yeah, but that's super helpful.
Yeah, okay, great.
Okay, so let's talk about what makes an event successful, and how you measure that.
So, to consider this, we'll look at that post-event survey that I've mentioned, we'll receive that direct feedback from our guests via a post-event survey.
And they can rate their overall experience as well as the individual aspects of the event, such as content, food and beverage, timing, et cetera.
This is really valuable firsthand information that we can use to improve our events moving forward based off of catering to what they want to see.
Attendance rate.
What was our attendance rate versus RSVP numbers?
What percentage actually showed up who RSVP'd?
Did they stay the whole time or did people leave early?
This is going to tell us before, during, and after the event if we are effectively positioning the event and if guests are seeing this as something that's valuable to their time.
Audience quality.
Who was actually at the event?
I know that the primary target is CMOs and VPs.
Are the people here, do they actually have those titles?
Are their industries and companies relevant?
We'll want to ensure that we're actually.
Reaching the target audience to ensure our events are beneficial to the company.
Engagement.
So were the guests engaged enough to share on social media?
Did our pre or post event content receive engagement?
This will tell us if we are offering shareable moments and correctly marketing our experiences.
Network growth.
Like you guys mentioned before, how many new attendees did we have who have not been to a growth X event before?
Did our network grow?
You know, connecting with these new individuals increases visibility and awareness.
Generation or conversion.
This is something that I will hopefully partner with you guys on and understanding how the team tracks this and measures this in a broader context as well as event specific.
And then stakeholder feedback, those stakeholder debriefs.
We discuss what went well, improvements to be made, what you enjoyed and would like to see again, and implement that feedback moving.
Moving.
forward.
Okay, so this is how I see my first 30 days on the roll going.
So I'll start with crash coursing, growthx, really understand the goals, the service, the product, and the audience here.

So meeting with stakeholders, understanding your priorities, what separates a good event from a great event, auditing, inventorying, and training on any pre-existing vendors, processes, systems, so that I understand the framework to really hit the ground running, and then creating that upcoming events calendar.
So securing any good dates, establishing blackout dates that don't align with your schedule, establishing those industry engagement opportunities, and any conflicts that we want to avoid.
Budget discussions, how we want to prioritize the budget, what's the most effective way to utilize what we have.
Team introductions, meeting.
Along with the team, really starting establishing those relationships, aligning on the culture.
So I really want to understand the culture so that I can bring that from an internal audience to an external audience through the events and experiences that we're creating.
And then, yeah, going ahead and starting work on the CMO breakfast, first executive dinner, and research and background on the user conference to hit the ground running.
Okay, thank you guys so much for bearing with me through that.
Like I said, I was really excited, enjoyed working on it, and happy to answer any questions about what we covered or myself or anything else you guys might have.
Yeah, like, I guess, like, how do you think about, like, the evolution of, like, what you could do and build?
And what I mean by that is, like, how do we build this in a way that over time becomes easier and...

You know, to, to execute and, and kind of nurture and kind of, you know, create the connections and things like that, or kind of like more of a, like that, that bigger picture of like, why are we doing all this kind of, right?
Like, like we know fundamentally, but, but like, just kind of how you think about it, like long term, you know, versus like executing, I guess, this roadmap more like the bigger picture, you know?
Yeah, absolutely.

Well, I think like, as you go and executing this initial roadmap, you'll start to build out processes that work really well, build out guidebooks for each event that you do, so that you understand like the baseline process, and it becomes more easy and templatable as you go.
So, I think it's, yeah, I think it's building out that initial infrastructure that always makes things easier as you go, you know?
I'm not sure how you guys track your invitations and your connections.
I've worked in Marketo.
Obviously to track all of that, but having that resource to access that information and sort through the audiences that are relevant to each event.
I think it's, yeah, it's building upon what you're already doing as you go and creating an easier process in the future.
Cool.
Jason, anything you're in?
Yeah, I mean, I...
Maybe after that one, just to make sure Amy had time to ask us questions.
Yeah, maybe the last one for me.
I guess you've seen these like four things we're thinking of doing, like the breakfast, you know, these joint things.
Like, do you have any thoughts in general of just that portfolio of events?
Do you agree with it?
Like, do you have any feedback for us for what events?
I point.
I think it's definitely a great starting point to like establish a general cadence of events.
I think that there are definitely cool things that you can add on to it.
For example, like as you like build out the client base.

And connections, I think that some sort of retreat would be really nice, maybe like every six months to really like you could execute the content and connection piece of the conference that you're hoping to do and do it in more of like an intimate like environment at a place where you guys can go and be together and enjoy your time intimately as well as giving that content outward.
So I think there's a lot of options that you can build upon upon this.
Cool.
I think that's all the questions I have.
Still have a few minutes to answer some questions if you have any.
Yeah, absolutely.
I'd love to know from your guys' point of view, like how events plays into your broader marketing strategy.
I mean, I guess I'll answer them, Marcel.
Feel free to chime in.
But I mean, so far, I want to say we just have conviction that like building relationships matter.
Um, like tactically, I mean, you know, like a private dinner where you're next to somebody for a couple hours is like a great way to like push things to the finish line.
Um, and we try to use that, you know, use it that way.
Um, so at the moment we kind of think about it as a way to kind of enable sales or just to facilitate kind of more, more deals in the pipeline, you know, or deals closing.
Um, I think going forward, we do want the events to be a little bit more of like an awareness builder for us, like a way to make it feel like growthx is a little bit more permanent.
You know, we stand for something and we're bringing people together and investing.
Um, and I guess, you know, if you ask me like what that drives, I guess, you know, it's kind of like a halo.
We have our service, we have a community, um, we'll eventually have a product and I guess we just have some.
Level of faith that that, you know, will contribute.
But I think that was kind of the question to you with like, you know, a bit more events background of like, how, how would you think about it?
Like, how do you prove ROI or even think about ROI for a lot of this stuff?
I'm not really sure.
It's a good question that I think events people think about a lot, you know, because events like Marcel was saying can scale up so quickly and become so expensive.
And, you know, I think it's really establishing like what those goals are, like you said, whether that's visibility or doing specific like sales and deal closing.
I think each event has different measurement opportunities.
And if it is that visibility and connection, then it potentially could just mean increasing your network by having new people there.
Or it could be, like you said, like the closing, initiating the closing of deals.
So definitely.
Okay, great.
And then I would just, sorry, go ahead.
Oh, no.
That was.
I was like, yeah, I guess we're still learning, honestly.
Yeah, yeah, of course.
And then I'd just love to hear a little bit more about what inspired you, Marcel, and starting GrowthX and the story there.
Yeah, yeah.
I think it's really like I started to build a lot of it internally in my last job.
And I think it's something that I've been already been building, meaning like helping brands think more like publishers and how to build, create content that builds trust with audiences.
And so then it was kind of like from there, starting to like do workshops, like I said, and then just kind of organically evolved.
And as we did that, realized like these AI workflows using existing tools were just not scaling.
And that's when we started to build like kind of our infrastructure and everything that we built to deliver things as a service to start with.
And I think that's why we're seeing so much traction is just.
It's that people kind of resonate with, like, solving my own pain kind of thing.
And we're selling what I wish I could buy, had bought before, instead of having to buy, you know, hire a bunch of people, a bunch of vendors, bunch of things.
And so there's this kind of very authentic thing of, like, people in our community, the people in our network that gravitate towards it.
And I try to share a lot of the more vulnerable parts of my story and things like that, like being laid off for fire multiple times.
And, you know, like, IVF journey, like things that kind of, like, you know, I've struggled with.
And so I think, like, people just connect a little bit more on the personal level.
And truly, like, a lot of people that are in our community, that some of them are customers, I kind of consider them friends.
And so it's like, it's just kind of like, we're just creating opportunities for us all to hang out and have a good time.
And then they just end up wanting to work with us.
And that's just a nice bonus on top a little bit, you know.
Amazing.
Yeah, I feel like it's always the best when you're solving your own problem to come up with something.
So that's awesome.

Well, I really appreciate all the work, and great job, and we're regrouping internally, but we try to move as fast as possible on our end as well, so I appreciate all the work you put in, and yeah, great job with everything.
Thank you guys so much for your time, really appreciate it, if you have a great rest of your days.
You too, take care, Amy.
You too, take care.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Panel interview with Amy Piazza for Events Marketing Manager role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Amy has 5 years of events experience, including work at Anthropic, Andreessen Horowitz, and production agencies](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=350.0)
  - [She presented a comprehensive events strategy and execution plan for GrowthX's current event types](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [Discussion focused on scaling events efficiently, measuring ROI, and evolving the events program over time](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=13.0)
  - [GrowthX leaders emphasized importance of relationship-building and creating awareness through events](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=394.0)

## Topics

### Amy's Background and Experience

  - [5 years in events industry, most recently at Anthropic on internal events team](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=354.0)
  - [3 years at Andreessen Horowitz working on Growth Fund events, executive dinners, LP summits](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=380.0)
  - [Early career in production at agencies like George P. Johnson and Creative Edge](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=403.0)
  - [Experience spans internal events, executive-level programs, and large-scale conferences](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=415.0)

### GrowthX Current Event Strategy

  - [Four main event types: CMO breakfasts (1-2 months), executive dinners (every couple months), joint happy hours, goal of user conference](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=428.0)
  - [Amy presented 6-month event calendar incorporating these events](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=461.0)
  - [Discussion on expanding audience and network through events, especially for breakfasts](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=625.0)

### Event Execution Process

  - [Pre-event: Stakeholder management, resource builds, venue/vendor coordination, budget tracking](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=510.0)
  - [Day-of: On-site management, vendor coordination, host support, content capture](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=557.0)
  - [Post-event: Thank yous, feedback collection, performance analysis, stakeholder debrief](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=586.0)

### Event Ideas and Recommendations

  - [Executive Dinners: Intimate roundtables with facilitated discussions and thought leadership](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=814.0)
  - [CMO Breakfasts: Scalable networking opportunity, potentially monthly "breakfast club" format](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=996.0)
  - [Joint Happy Hours: 2-hour post-conference events to build reputation and industry engagement](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=1225.0)
  - [User Conference: 6-hour format with sessions and networking, focus on education/innovation](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=1535.0)

### Measuring Event Success

  - [Post-event surveys for direct feedback](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=1970.0)
  - [Attendance rates and audience quality metrics](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2003.0)
  - [Engagement on social media and with event content](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2048.0)
  - [Network growth and new attendee acquisition](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2064.0)
  - [Lead generation or deal conversion (to be defined with team)](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2078.0)
  - [Stakeholder feedback and debrief sessions](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2090.0)

### Budget and ROI Considerations

  - [Discussion on balancing event scale/production with ROI](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=1640.0)
  - [Exploring ways to create valuable experiences without excessive costs](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=1867.0)
  - [Importance of defining clear goals and metrics for each event type](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=1847.0)

### Long-Term Events Strategy

  - [Building repeatable processes and templates to increase efficiency over time](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2299.0)
  - [Potential to add new event types like client retreats as program evolves](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2334.0)
  - [Focus on creating awareness and "halo effect" for GrowthX brand](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2429.0)
  - [Balancing relationship-building with measurable business impact](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2391.0)

## Next Steps

  - [GrowthX team to regroup internally and make decision on hiring](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2202.0)
  - [If hired, Amy to begin with deep dive into GrowthX business, goals, and existing processes](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2110.0)
  - [Create detailed upcoming events calendar and budget allocation plan](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2140.0)
  - [Initiate planning for next CMO breakfast and executive dinner events](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=2176.0)
  - [Begin research and background work for potential user conference](https://fathom.video/share/FPeHdpnrGrnL5mDhpw9n3y5QsMKf23VD?tab=summary&timestamp=1535.0)

action_items.description: Create comprehensive workbook for CMO breakfast (incl. schedule, attendee list, agenda, seating chart, budget tracking)
Build 8-week workback calendar for next executive dinner
Research & propose venue options for executive dinners (e.g. Oficina, Birdsong, Marianne, Speakeasy, 95 Federal)
Create proposal for recurring monthly CMO breakfast club (e.g. 1st/2nd Tuesday)
Develop "bring a friend" strategy for CMO breakfasts to expand network
Finalize details for upcoming happy hour at 25 Lusk (photo booth, swag wheel, entertainment options)
Research agency partners for user conference (e.g. Glow, Grow, MSI agencies)
Draft 30-day onboarding plan (incl. GrowthX crash course, stakeholder meetings, vendor/process audit, budget discussions)
Schedule meeting w/ Amy to discuss budget priorities & most effective utilization
